conbody.p: In this guide, we will provide a simple, three-step path, to quickly get you up and running with Juniper Cloud-Native Router (JCNR) on Red Hat® OpenShift® Container Platform (OCP). We'll show you how to install and configure the Cloud-Native Router release 23.2 as a container network interface (CNI) on the worker nodes of an OCP cluster to provide Layer 3 connectivity between customer workloads. Depending on the complexity of your design, other tasks may be required in addition to the ones included in this workflow.

section.title: Meet Juniper Cloud-Native Router
section.p: The Juniper Cloud-Native Router (JCNR) is a container-based software solution that combines the JCNR-controller (cRPD-based control plane) and the Cloud-Native Router vRouter (DPDK-enabled forwarding/data plane). With the cloud-native router, you can enable Junos OS-based switching control with enhanced forwarding capabilities. The Cloud-Native Router controller, running on a Kubernetes (K8s) compute host, provides control plane management functionality and uses the forwarding capabilities provided by either the Linux kernel or the DPDK-enabled Cloud-Native Router vRouter. DPDK is an open source set of libraries and drivers. It enables fast packet processing by allowing network interface cards (NICs) to send direct memory access (DMA) packets directly into an application's address space. The applications poll for packets to avoid the overhead of interrupts from the NIC. Integrating the DPDK allows a vRouter to process more packets per second than is possible when the vRouter runs as a kernel module. In this integrated solution, the Cloud-Native Router Controller uses gPRC-based services to exchange messages and to communicate with the Cloud-Native Router vRouter, thus creating a fully functional Cloud Native Router. This close communication allows you to learn about fabric and workload interfaces, provision DPDK or kernel-based interfaces for K8s pods as needed and configure IPv4 and IPv6 address allocation for pods.

section.title: Get Ready
section.p: Cloud-Native Router is supported on the following OpenShift platforms:
Ensure you have a valid Cloud-Native Router license. You can request for a license key from the JAL portal.

li.p: Download the Cloud-Native Router tarball, Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_release_nunber.tgz from the Juniper Support Site, to a directory on your host machine. You must perform the file transfer in binary mode when transferring the file to your server, so that the compressed tar file expands properly.

note.p: All remaining steps in the installation assume that your current working directory is now Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_<release-number>.

li.p: View the contents in the current directory.
li.codeblock: ls contrail-tools helmchart images README.md secrets

li.p: Upload the Cloud-Native Router images to local docker using the docker load command.

Enter the root password for your host server and your Juniper Cloud-Native Router license file into the secrets/jcnr-secrets.yaml file. You must enter the password and license in base64 encoded format. Apply the secrets.
li.codeblock: kubectl apply -f secrets/jcnr-secrets.yaml namespace/jcnr created secret/jcnr-secrets created

li.p: Customize the helm chart for your deployment using the helmchart/values.yaml file. You must configure the fabric interfaces and the number of replicas based on your setup. The cni_bin_dir must be configured for OCP. A sample values.yaml file for OCP is provided below.
li.codeblock:
global: registry: enterprise-hub.juniper.net/
common: vrouter: repository: atom-docker/cn2/bazel-build/dev/ tag: R23.2-156 crpd: repository: junos-docker-local/warthog/ tag: 23.2R1.14 jcnrcni: repository: junos-docker-local/warthog/ tag: 23.2-20230628-8cf4350
replicas: "2"
fabricInterface: - ens1f0 - ens1f1 log_level: "DEBUG" log_path: "/var/log/jcnr/" syslog_notifications: "/var/log/jcnr/jcnr_notifications.json" nodeAffinity: - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/worker operator: Exists - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/master operator: DoesNotExist
cni_bin_dir: /var/lib/cni/bin
jcnr-vrouter: restoreInterfaces: false mtu: "9000" cpu_core_mask: "2,3" ddp: true qosEnable: false multinode: true corePattern: "/var/crash/core-%e.%p.%h.%t" coreFilePath: /var/crash vrouter_dpdk_uio_driver: "vfio-pci"

li.p: Deploy the Juniper Cloud-Native Router using the helm chart.Navigate to the helmchart directory and run the command provided below.
li.codeblock: NAME: jcnr LAST DEPLOYED: Fri Jun 23 06:04:33 2023 NAMESPACE: default STATUS: deployed REVISION: 1 TEST SUITE: None
NAME NAMESPACE REVISION UPDATED STATUS CHART APP VERSION jcnr default 1 2023-06-23 06:04:33.144611017 -0400 EDT deployed jcnr-23.2.0 23.2.0

li.p: Ensure the Cloud-Native Router pods are running.
li.codeblock: contrail-deploy contrail-k8s-deployer-6489b59fd6-5r8lb 1/1 Running 0 4d3h 192.168.10.111 ocp-worker1 <none> <none> contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes-6gwnq 3/3 Running 0 4d3h 192.168.10.111 ocp-worker1 <none> <none> contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes-kjchz 3/3 Running 0 4d3h 192.168.10.114 ocp-worker2 <none> <none> jcnr kube-crpd-worker-sts-0 1/1 Running 0 4d3h 192.168.10.111 ocp-worker1 <none> <none> jcnr kube-crpd-worker-sts-1 1/1 Running 0 4d3h 192.168.10.114 ocp-worker2 <none> <none> jcnr syslog-ng-fzkrv 1/1 Running 0 4d3h 192.168.10.114 ocp-worker2 <none> <none> jcnr syslog-ng-hnxx4 1/1 Running 0 4d3h 192.168.10.111 ocp-worker1 <none> <none>
